title: Truth and Discernment
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Truth and Discernment - Philippians 1_9-11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Introduction: The ongoing series on biblical/spiritual discernment
Paul's prayer: love abounding in knowledge and all discernment
The connection between love and discernment
Approving the things that are excellent
Being sincere and without offense until the day of Christ
Filled with the fruits of righteousness through Jesus Christ
Conclusion: True discernment as a fruit of love and knowledge
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the Discernment series from Philippians 1:9-11, where Paul prays that love would abound in knowledge and all discernment. He shows that true biblical discernment is rooted in love and produces the ability to approve what is excellent and bear the fruits of righteousness.
transcript:
## The Definition and Foundation of Discernment

We are in a series on biblical discernment. Hebrews 5:14 tells us that discernment is a skill — something acquired and grown as we mature. The definition we have been working with:

> *Discernment is the acquired skill of understanding and applying God's word with the purpose of separating truth from error, and right from wrong, and living according to what is right.*

Discernment includes both thinking rightly and living according to what we know. God is not interested in people who merely know what is right; he wants people who do what is right. James says that faith without works is dead, and that we must be doers of the word, not merely hearers.

## Truth Exists and Can Be Known

To practice discernment — to separate truth from error, right from wrong — you must first be convinced that **truth exists** and can be known. This is not self-evident in our culture. But the Bible is clear.

In John 8:31-32, Jesus says to those who believe him:

> *"If you abide in My word, you are My disciples indeed. And you shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free."* (John 8:31-32)

Notice the connection: knowing the truth is tied to abiding in the word of Christ. And note the article: not *a* truth among many possibilities, but *the* truth — objective truth that exists independently of us.

This is directly opposed to the dominant mindset of our day. Something is not good simply because we like it, or evil simply because it conflicts with what we feel. Something is objectively true or false, good or evil, because God says so — regardless of our preferences or experiences.

Psalm 119:160: *"The entirety of Your word is truth."* Colossians 1:5 speaks of "the word of the truth of the gospel." Paul instructs Timothy to be "a worker who does not need to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth" (2 Timothy 2:15).

The most dangerous errors are not the obvious ones far out on the fringe. The most dangerous error is the **counterfeit** — the error that most closely resembles the truth, because it takes genuine discernment to identify it. A crayon drawing of a hundred dollar bill is no one's concern. A near-perfect forgery is. The most effective lies stay close enough to the truth that the casual reader will not notice.

## The Source of Truth

In John 14:6, Jesus says: *"I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me."* Christ himself is truth in its fullest form. John 1:14: *"The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory... full of grace and truth."*

Truth is not God — but God is the source of all truth. Therefore discernment apart from Jesus Christ is impossible. Christians should be the most discerning people in the world.

How do we know if something is morally true? Two tests that must be met together: Does the Bible teach it? And does it honor Christ? These two cannot be separated. Something may appear to have biblical support and yet not honor Christ — which means the biblical support was a misreading. Both tests must be satisfied.

This is what Paul is praying for the Philippians in our passage:

> *"This I pray, that your love may abound still more and more in knowledge and all discernment, that you may approve the things that are excellent, that you may be sincere and without offense till the day of Christ, being filled with the fruits of righteousness which are by Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God."* (Philippians 1:9-11)

Love abounding in knowledge and discernment, so that we may *approve the things that are excellent* — that is, discern what is true and good — and do so to the glory and praise of God. That is the goal.

## The Subtlety of Error

A dangerous mindset among many Christians is: *repeated exposure to just a little bit of evil will not harm me — I can build up a resistance.* This is a lie. The closer you move to the line of sin, the more the line moves. We renegotiate our boundaries based on how close we have already moved, and call the new position safe.

A ship beginning a 6,000-mile voyage that is just half a degree off course will not notice the deviation for a long time. But by journey's end, it will be miles and miles from its destination. Small, consistent deviations from truth and obedience accumulate. Error may be subtle, but it is always deadly.

## Navigating Gray Areas

Biblical discernment is not only about the obvious black-and-white questions. Sometimes situations appear genuinely gray. Six principles help navigate these areas:

**1. Rarity.** Truly gray situations are rare. Most situations that appear gray are gray only because we have not done enough biblical thinking, or because our own desires have inserted themselves between us and clarity. Often we prefer the gray area because we do not want to obey what God clearly commands.

**2. The Fall.** Grayness is always a consequence of the Fall. In a perfect world, everything would be clear. Gray areas exist because creation is broken.

**3. Clarity.** When dealing with gray areas, begin with what the Bible makes clear. Do not allow a rare or unusual case to overturn a clear biblical principle. Marriage, for example, is clearly defined by Scripture as between one man and one woman. We do not establish a general rule by working backwards from an extreme exception.

**4. Humility.** When we reach the limits of our understanding, gray areas teach us that we are creatures with limited perspective and wisdom. That humility is itself a gift.

**5. Dependence.** Gray areas give us the opportunity to express our dependence on the Spirit of God. Any ability we have in spiritual discernment is a gift from him.

**6. Conscience.** While conscience must be subservient to Scripture and informed by it, it plays an important role in the Christian life and should not be ignored — especially in situations where the biblical path is not immediately obvious.

## The Goal

As we grow in our knowledge of God and of truth, we grow in our ability to practice biblical discernment — separating truth from error, right from wrong, and then living accordingly. Paul prays that this love, discernment, and resulting righteousness would all be "to the glory and praise of God." That is the end: not merely a more accurate worldview, not merely better decisions, but the glory of God in our lives and in the world.
